Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal The Record ? Crc Rule 2.550
Off Calendar Sep-08-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF JANE DOE'S Motion To Seal The Record CRC Rule 2.550:

Plaintiff's unopposed to seal portions of the record in this case is granted in part as follows. Those portions of the following pleadings that contain specific references to dates and descriptions of incidents that correspond to dates and incidents mentioned in the parties' dissolution of marriage complaint in San Mateo may be filed under seal, so as to avoid disclosing the parties' identities.
1. The complaint;
2. Defendant's motion for leave to file first amended answer; and
3. Defendant's separate statement of disputed responses to form interrogatories, special interrogatories and requests for production.

The parties shall lodge redacted versions of these documents for filing.

The Court finds that there exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the portions of the records to be sealed; the overriding interest supports sealing those records; a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest.

Prevailing/Moving party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and including the e-filing transaction #s and filed dates of the documents to be sealed via email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

Plaintiff Jane Doe'S Motion To Quash (Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling)
Off Calendar Sep-02-2020 Off Calendar

Motion And Notice Of Motion To Quash
Off Calendar Sep-02-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Hannah Salassi for Plaintiff.
Thomas Palmer for Defendant.

THE PARTIES STIPULATE TO THE MATTER BEING DECIDED BY THE JUDGE PRO TEM. THE PARTIES AND THE JUDGE PRO TEM APPEARING REMOTELY AUTHORIZE THE CLERK TO SIGN THE FORMAL STIPULATION FORM ON THEIR BEHALF.

ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFF JANE DOE's Motion To Quash:

The California Constitution guarantees all people certain inalienable rights, among them the right of "pursuing and obtaining . . . privacy." Cal. Const., art. 1, Sec. 1. The right of privacy extends to information in an individual's medical records, information about his or her physical and mental condition, and information in his or her medical history. See John B. v. Superior Court, 38 Cal. 4th 1177, 1198-99 (2006); Hill v. National Collegiate
Athletic Assn., 7 Cal.4th 1, 41 (1994); Board of Medical Quality Assurance v. Gherardini, 93 Cal. App. 3d 669, 678-679 (1979). "The right to privacy, however, is not absolute." John B. v. Superior Court, 38 Cal. 4th 1177, 1199 (2006). In appropriate circumstances, this right must be balanced against other important interests. See Hill v. National Collegiate Athletic Assn., 7 Cal. 4th 1, 37 (1994). "On occasion [a party's] privacy interests may have to give way to [the] opponent's right to a fair trial. Thus courts must balance the right of civil litigants to discover relevant facts against the privacy interests of persons subject to discovery." Vinson v. Superior Court, 43 Cal. 3d 833, 842 (1987). "The burden is on the party seeking the constitutionally protected information to establish direct relevance" of the evidence sought. Davis, 7 Cal. App. 4th at 1017. "Mere speculation as to the possibility that some portion of the records might be relevant to some substantive issue does not suffice" to overcome the medical record privilege. Id.

Therefore, Plaintiff's motion to quash is granted in part and denied in part, and the request for sanctions is denied. The subpoena itself is crafted so broadly to seek all medical records during a 17 year period, which is clearly too broad. But, as Defendant establishes, Defendant is entitled to medical records that go back as far as 2004 that speak to the allegations in the complaint. Thus the subpoena in its current form is quashed, but it is without prejudice to Defendant issuing a more narrowly tailored subpoena that seeks medical records that would lead to the discovery of admissible evidence of the incidents that Plaintiff claims give rise to liability in this matter. Given the allegations in this case, records dating back to at least 2004 do not appear to be too remote.

Prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312 via email to jyjudgeprotem@gmail.com.
Judge Pro Tem: Jason Yurasek, Clerk: S. Le, Not reported. =(302/JPT)
